ambient_lines.text: Stellaris Port. I used to know every docking clerk by name. Doubt any of them remember me.
Guild territory. Say what you will about the bureaucracy. Their docking procedures are impeccable.
I've been running fuel projections. We could save twelve percent if we adjusted our departure windows.
The navigation charts for this region haven't been updated in three years. I'll draft corrections when we dock.
Jin, did you re-torque the port coupling? I can feel a 0.3-degree drift.
Drifter, if you're going to renegotiate our fuel contract, at least tell me first. I had the routing planned around the old price.
Breakstone. The ore dust never really washes off, does it?
Union steel. You can always tell by the welds. Clean, even, no shortcuts.
Engine's running clean. Whoever built this ship knew what they were doing. Mostly.
I've been thinking about replacing the secondary coolant line. It's within spec, but just barely.
Doc, your spectrometer's pulling 3 amps over rated. I'll swap the capacitor tonight.
Reeves, those departure calculations you sent. Good work. Saved us twenty minutes on the approach.
Axiom Labs. The Collective's flagship research station. I had a corner office once. The view was terrible.
Collective space. The data networks here are exceptional. If they'd only share them more freely.
I've been cataloging the mineral traces in our last haul. Three compounds I've never seen in combination before.
Every system we visit adds to the dataset. Patterns emerge if you're patient enough to look.
Tomas, I've been analyzing your trade routes. There's a fascinating pattern in how you avoid Collective checkpoints.
Marcus, the harmonic frequency on the port engine shifted 0.2 hertz. Might want to check the mounting bolts.
Haven's Rest. My mother ran a noodle stand on Deck Seven. Best laksa in the sector.
Alliance space. The tariffs are low, the paperwork is thin, and nobody asks where you've been. My kind of port.
You know what I miss? Real weather. Station air gets old after a while.
I heard there's a seller at the next port who owes me a favor. Might get us a better rate on fuel cells.
Reeves, you ever loosen up? I've seen navigation charts with more personality.
Doc, you ever think about selling those mineral analyses? Some traders would pay good credits for that data.
Nexus Prime. I used to run the loading bays on Deck Twelve. Tightest cargo stacking in the sector.
Guild docks are always organized. Makes my job easier when everything's labeled right.
I rearranged the aft hold. Got us another eight cubic meters. You're welcome.
Stellaris Port. The trading floor here sets the tone for the whole sector. Watch the boards.
Guild stations post their price data honestly, at least. Makes forecasting possible.
Crystal prices are diverging from the three-month trend. Could mean opportunity, could mean trouble.
The Reach. Good place to disappear. Better place to stay sharp.
Unaffiliated space. Keep weapons hot. Nobody's coming to help out here.
Weapons are clean, sights are calibrated. When it counts, we won't miss.
Forgeworks. The vibration here is different from anywhere else. Foundries never sleep.
Union stations build their shields to last. Overbuilt, the Guild says. I call it reliable.
I tweaked the shield harmonics again. Two percent better absorption across the board.
Breakstone. My grandfather drilled Shaft Four. My father drilled Shaft Seven. This place is in my bones.
Union rock, Union hands. Nobody drills cleaner.
Drill heads need replacing every forty shifts. Most people don't think about it until it's too late.
Iron Depths. DCMC runs a tight operation down here. Tight and quiet.
Union mining stations always have the best geological surveys. The data is thorough.
The color of the fracture line tells you everything. Most people just see rock.
Haven's Rest. I mapped my first route from this dock. Fuel depot to Verdant, the long way around.
Alliance space. The routes out here weren't charted by committee. They were found by people who needed them.
There's a fuel-saving shortcut through this region. Most pilots miss it because the charts are outdated.
Verdant. Greenest station in the sector. The air recyclers here actually work properly.
Alliance territory. I know these patrol routes. We're safe here. Mostly.
Sensors are reading clean ahead, but I've been wrong before. Stay alert.
Crimson Reach. Nobody asks names here. That's the appeal.
Inspection coming up. Relax. The compartments are clean. They're always clean.
The less you know about what I did before this, the better we both sleep.
Nexus Prime. I used to stamp manifests in that customs office. Now I unstamp them.
Guild paperwork is thorough. That's what makes it fun to forge.
I updated the cargo manifests. Everything looks perfectly ordinary. As intended.
Axiom Labs. Collective stations always smell like synthesized coffee. I've missed it.
Collective space. The sensor arrays here are first-rate. I can calibrate remotely.
I've been running the scan data through a new filter. There's something in the background noise worth looking at.
Nova Research. Half the labs here are classified. The other half should be.
Collective stations archive everything. Somewhere in those databanks is something nobody's looked at twice.
That debris field we passed had organic traces. I took samples. You'll thank me later.
Haven's Rest. The community councils here actually listen. It's refreshing.
Alliance space. People here judge you by what you do, not who you work for.
I smoothed things over with the dockmaster. We should get priority clearance next time.
Stellaris Port. I negotiated my first contract in that building. The seller never stood a chance.
Guild stations price fairly, but fairly isn't the same as cheaply. Leave the talking to me.
I've been reviewing our last three trades. We left credits on the table. Won't happen again.
The Fulcrum. The corridors are too clean. Too quiet. I don't miss it.
Perimeter is secure. I checked twice.
If we're going groundside, I want point. Non-negotiable.
I keep a list of every ship I couldn't save. It's shorter than you'd think. That's the part that bothers me.
I skipped the medical again. I know. The ship needs me more than the clinic does.
This sector gives me a bad feeling. The kind I get right before something expensive breaks.
People always assume the worst thing I did was violent. It wasn't. I just walked away when someone needed me to stay.
I don't sleep well out here. The quiet sounds wrong when you're used to shipyard noise.
Check your seals twice before we jump. I've pulled bodies out of decompressed compartments. You don't forget the faces.
The Collective offered me my position back last month. Better lab, better pay. I deleted the message.
Statistically, this route should have killed us twice by now. I find that oddly motivating.
I keep a pressed sample from every system we visit. Dust, pollen, metal shavings. My collection fills a drawer now.
My mother died on a station that couldn't afford medicine. So yeah, I have opinions about trade embargo policy.
I've been in cantinas on eleven stations. They all smell the same. Recycled air, cheap synth-alcohol, and desperation.
People look at me and see a charmer. I look at me and see someone who learned that smiling is cheaper than fighting.
Jin, you ever wonder if we're the only ones out here who fix things instead of strip them for parts?
Drifter, you talk your way out of everything. One day that won't work. I hope I'm there when it happens.
Elena, when was the last time you ate something that wasn't a ration bar? Your cortisol readings are concerning.
Doc, I've seen you stare at data for nine hours straight. You lecture me about self-care?
I count the crates. Every time. My last crew didn't count. That's why they're my last crew.
Nexus Prime charges by the cubic centimeter for hold space. You learn to pack tight or you go hungry.
The numbers always tell a story. Lately the story is that someone's manipulating the crystal market. And they're good at it.
I got into market analysis because I like certainty. Funny place to look for it.
I stopped counting kills after Shaft Seven. The number doesn't help you sleep.
The Reach taught me one thing: everybody's armed, and half of them are scared. Scared people miss. Angry people don't.
I name my tools. The torque wrench is Patience. The arc welder is Last Resort. Don't ask about the hammer.
Forgeworks foundry workers get a fifteen-minute break every six hours. I used to spend mine just breathing clean air.
My grandfather's lungs gave out at fifty-two. My father's at forty-eight. I hum his song when I drill. Keeps the count steady.
You ever feel the rock breathe? Deep enough, the pressure changes are rhythmic. Almost like a heartbeat.
DCMC doesn't publish the real incident numbers. I've seen the internal reports. The published ones are... optimistic.
Every ore sample is a grave marker for something that lived millions of years ago. I try to remember that.
I mapped a route once that saved two days of fuel. The pilot who flew it found a debris field that wasn't on any chart. Wasn't a good day.
The old charts have gaps. Not errors. Gaps. Places someone decided you shouldn't go. I keep a list.
Sensors can't see everything. I trust the instruments, but I also trust the knot in my stomach. Both have saved my life.
I scouted for a convoy once that never arrived at the rendezvous. I waited three days. The beacon's still there.
You're wondering about the name. Keep wondering.
I had a real name once. It's on a warrant somewhere. Doesn't matter anymore.
The Guild thinks identity is a number in a database. I think identity is whatever you can make someone believe.
I forged my first document at sixteen. A meal voucher. You do what you have to when the ration line closes at noon.
The Collective let me go because I asked questions about Project Terminus. They said the project didn't exist. My clearance said otherwise.
I keep a journal in a cipher nobody else can read. Habit from the labs. Paranoia, maybe. But useful paranoia.
I keep a pressed flower from Verdant in my kit. The species went extinct two years after I collected it. Last one anywhere.
Half the things I study, nobody's looked at twice. The other half, somebody looked at once and classified. There's a pattern there.
Diplomacy is just listening until someone tells you what they actually want. Most people are too busy talking to hear it.
I brokered a ceasefire on Haven's Rest once. Took eleven hours. Both sides thanked me. Neither side meant it.
The best deal I ever made cost someone their livelihood. I didn't know until after. I don't make deals like that anymore.
Everyone thinks negotiation is about winning. It's about knowing what losing looks like and making the other person see it first.
The Fulcrum trains you to sleep in four-hour blocks. I can't do eight anymore. Doesn't feel safe.
I left the military because I started agreeing with orders I shouldn't have. That scared me more than combat ever did.
That margin was razor-thin. I hope you factored in docking fees.
We could have held that inventory another cycle and cleared twenty percent more.
Good timing on the buy. The spread favors us.
That is a lot of cargo for our hold. I assume you have a buyer lined up.
We survived. Run the damage assessment before we celebrate.
Smart. Fighting was not worth the repair bill.
Contract fulfilled. I have already logged the payout.
That deadline was always unrealistic. I should have said something sooner.
I did not see that cargo. As far as the manifest is concerned, neither did you.
I told you the inspection schedules are posted publicly. You chose not to check.
Credits are credits. Just keep the fuel tank funded.
Heavy load. The struts can handle it, but I would rather they did not have to.
Clean win. I will patch the hull scoring before it oxidizes.
We hit hard. That felt right.
Running. Again.
I understand why we ran. I just do not have to like it.
Job done. Next one.
We missed the window. Could have used better planning on that one.
Everyone walked out. That is the only metric that counts.
That went sideways fast. We need to debrief before the next drop.
Profitable. I assume the provenance of that cargo was legitimate.
Interesting purchase. I would like to run some scans before we stow it.
Violence resolved it. I wish I could say I was surprised.
Discretion. Thank you for choosing it.
Well executed. The data from this run will be useful.
A missed deadline is not a failure if we learned something. But we should learn faster.
I have concerns about what we just transported. I would like to discuss them when you have a moment.
Caught. I cannot say I am entirely unsympathetic to the inspectors on this one.
Sold at the right moment. I taught you that, you know.
Buying in bulk. Bold. I like bold.
We won. Could have talked our way out, but winning works too.
Retreating is just negotiating with physics. No shame in it.
Another satisfied client. I will make sure they remember our name.
Expired. I had a contact who could have extended the deadline. Should have mentioned it.
Smooth delivery. The kind nobody writes down.
Caught. I know a lawyer on Nexus Prime. Well, I know someone who knows a lawyer.
Good sale. I have the hold swept and ready for the next load.
Heavy shipment. I will rebalance the weight distribution.
Guns ran clean. Good engagement.
We broke off. Next time, let me line up the shot.
Shields held. Barely. I am recalibrating the emitters now.
Good yield from that run. The drill heads are holding up.
Clean run. Nobody saw anything because there was nothing to see.
Sloppy. I told you to check the patrol rotations.
Ground team performed well. Controlled aggression, clean execution.
We lost control of the situation down there. I want to review our approach before the next drop.
I left Stellaris because I stopped trusting my own calculations. They were right. I was the variable I could not account for.
You run this ship like you trust the people on it. That is either the smartest or the dumbest thing I have seen out here.
I have been aboard long enough to stop planning my exit route. That scares me. But it is the good kind of scared.
If you ever need someone to hold the ship together while you do something reckless, I will be at the console. Just do not make it a habit.
My father worked the same mine for forty years. Never complained once. I used to think that was strength. Now I think he was just afraid.
You asked me once why I fight so hard for this ship. It is because it is the first place that felt like it was worth fighting for.
I do not say this to many people. You are the captain I would have wanted back when it mattered most.
If we go down, we go down swinging. That is not bravado. It is a promise.
I was offered my old research chair back. Twice. I turned it down both times without telling you. I suppose I am telling you now.
Science taught me to question everything. This crew taught me that some things do not need questioning. They just need defending.
You let me be the person I chose to be instead of the person my credentials said I should be. I have never thanked you properly for that.
I keep a journal. Scientific habit. The entries from before I joined this crew are clinical. The recent ones are not. I think that means something.
Every port I have ever docked at, I left someone behind. This is the first ship where I stopped counting the exits.
I joke about the embargo because it is easier than remembering what my mother looked like before the rationing started.
I have been honest with you more than anyone else in the Expanse. That terrifies me. But I keep doing it, so make of that what you will.
If something happens to me, there is a lockbox on Haven's Rest. Third warehouse, north wall. The combination is my mother's birthday. You will know what to do with what is inside.
Jin, you bypassed two safety interlocks on the starboard coupling. I found them this morning. Disabled. Deliberately.
Marcus. That repair you did on the fuel line. It holds, but if it fails, it fails catastrophically. I need you to care about the difference between 'works' and 'safe.'
Reeves, your maintenance checklist has forty-seven items for a thruster swap. Forty-seven. I can do it in three steps. Four if I'm being careful.
Elena, you ran six simulations before we docked. Six. The dock was empty. Sometimes I think you'd simulate breathing if you could.
Priya, your sensor sweeps are pulling power from navigation. I understand the science matters to you, but so does not flying into an asteroid.
Doctor, I appreciate the research, but when you reroute processing power to your analysis without asking, we all feel it in the response lag.
Elena, you marked that anomaly as 'navigational obstruction' in the log. It was a previously undocumented stellar phenomenon. There is a difference.
Drifter, you changed our docking window without telling me. Again. I cannot plan around someone who treats schedules like suggestions.
Reeves, you filed a deviation report because I took a shortcut through the shipping lane. A shortcut. We saved forty minutes.
Doc, you wanted us to hold fire on that raider because they 'might be desperate.' They were shooting at us. Desperate people still kill you.
Priya, I respect the ethics. I do. But when someone has a gun on our hull, your ethics are a luxury we cannot afford.
Marcus, your first answer to every problem is force. Sometimes the wrench is not the right tool.
Jin, I counted. In the last six encounters, you suggested we fight in five of them. I would like you to consider what that pattern says.
Drifter, I watched you lie to that dock clerk's face and she thanked you for it. I do not know whether to be impressed or worried.
Jin, not every situation needs a fist. Some of them just need the right words. I know that offends you, but it is true.
Marcus, you broke that man's wrist over a docking dispute. A docking dispute. I had it handled.
Tomas, I found the manifest you altered. The one that moved restricted cargo through a Collective checkpoint. We need to talk about this.
Drifter, you told that station agent we were a relief ship. We are not a relief ship. Words have consequences.
Doc, you reported our own ship for an emissions irregularity. Our own ship. To a faction that would have fined us. I am still processing that.
Rask, your trigger discipline on the last run was sloppy. We are not militia. Tighten up.
Harkov, you field-stripped my sidearm and reassembled it without asking. I do not care if yours is better. Do not touch my equipment.
Okeke, your drill lubricant leaked into hold three again. I had to move six crates. At 0300.
Kai, if you stack crates on my drill housing one more time, I am welding the hold divider shut.
Petrov, your forgery on that transit permit was obvious. The seal weight was wrong. If I noticed, inspectors will too.
Zero, I have been forging documents since before you learned which end of a scanner to point at cargo. Save the critique.
Tova, your sample storage is contaminating my observation instruments. Biological material and precision optics do not mix.
Liang, you vented my culture samples because they were 'emitting particulates.' Those were spore patterns. I was studying them.
Jin, I still think you take too many risks. But I have stopped worrying you will not come back from them.
Reeves, your forty-seven step checklist saved us last week. I am not going to say that twice, so remember it.
Tomas, I disapprove of your methods. But I have noticed you never lie about things that matter. That counts for something.
Doc, I used to think you were naive. Turns out you just have a longer view of consequences than the rest of us. I am still catching up.
Priya, I still think you hesitate too long. But last time you hesitated, you were right and I was wrong. So.
Jin, you are the worst liar I have ever met. That is why I trust you.
Good vein. Keep the drill steady, the iron runs deep here.
Watch the pressure on that crystal. Hit it wrong and you lose half the yield.
That rock had good grain. I can tell from up here. You are getting an eye for it.
Deeper is better ore, but the walls get unpredictable. Stay sharp.
New depth, new composition. I will recalibrate the drill heads.
These scan readings suggest item density is higher on the port side. Worth investigating.
Careful with that panel. The corrosion pattern suggests structural compromise.
I am reading residual energy signatures behind that bulkhead. Could be worth a scan.
Hull integrity on this deck is marginal. Extract what we need and get out.
Extraction clean. I have it secured in the hold.
Forge is running hot. Good batch. The alloy composition is holding.
Output quality is above spec. Whatever you are doing with those recipes, keep doing it.
That output has a buyer at Stellaris Port. Just saying.
Processing complete. I have updated the manifest. We should talk about margins when you have a moment.
Stellaris Port. I know the bid patterns on this floor better than most people know their own family. Six years of logs.
The tells are consistent across price tiers. People think the high-value lots are different. They aren't.
Ceiling came in where I said it would. The pattern was clear from the third counter.
Guild stations post their trading data honestly, which means I can cross-reference the bids against the public record. Most people don't.
Reeves, you track fuel projections to the decimal. I track bid timing to the second. We would get along if either of us had more patience for the other's work.
Haven's Rest. The Alliance politics here run on obligation. Who owes whom, going back three administrations. Takes time to map.
Two confirmed, three soft, two I haven't reached yet. The count is where I expected it to be at this stage.
The vote isn't where the dispute is decided. The vote is the announcement. The work happens earlier.
Alliance space. The frontier settlements out here have been making deals without the Guild's paperwork for forty years. The obligation networks are dense.
Drifter, you know how to move in and out of a room without committing to anything. I respect that. It is a different skill than mine, but I respect it.
The Reach. Most of the disputes I worked out here were never filed. That was the point.
The question I ask in every session is the same: does this hold in three months? Most people are still arguing about who was right.
Both parties left the table unhappy. That usually means the outcome will hold. Satisfaction is a bad sign in mediation.
Doc, you approach data the way I approach a dispute: look for what the parties are not saying before you decide what they mean. We work similarly.
Jin, I appreciate that you fix things so they stay fixed. That is exactly what good mediation does. Different substrate, same principle.
Nexus Prime. I ran the Level Four desk for eleven years. The contracts here haven't changed. The people running them think they have.
The band was eighteen to twenty-four. It closed at twenty-one. Within range.
I can give you the range. I cannot give you the number. Anyone who gives you the number is guessing.
Guild floor here tracks the underlying data cleanly. Makes it easier to narrow the band when you have clean inputs.
Reeves, you give fuel projections as ranges with buffer. I give contract positions as ranges with confidence. We are doing the same job in different domains.
Axiom Labs. I reviewed proposals here for twelve years before I stopped letting the Collective decide which ones were worth funding.
The risk estimate on the last proposal was padded. The researcher knew the actual variance was higher. I asked for the real number.
Collective stations share their research data freely between member institutions. That part of the system still works.
Doc, walk me through the protocol on that mineral analysis, not the results. I want to see where the assumptions are.
Jin, I looked at your engine maintenance logs. Your methodology is sound. Every repair documented, every variance noted. That is the discipline that makes a project hold up.
The Guild docking fee schedule here runs in 15-minute increments. I memorized the rate table my first year. It is still accurate.
The NAS antenna array at Stellaris runs three channels I was not supposed to know about. The pricing signals are cleaner if you factor them in.
Section Twelve went cold last winter. Pipe failure on the primary heat exchanger. Nobody in maintenance had read the maintenance contract.
The Silent Shaft memorial went up the year after I left. I never saw the dedication. Someone sent me a photograph. I keep it with the Section Nine incident reports.
The Institute publication review cycle is documented at ninety days. In practice it runs a hundred and forty. The gap is never on record anywhere.
The Uprising data the Collective keeps at Axiom is categorized as historical incident analysis. The recategorization happened in the same month I started asking questions.
Way I see it, Haven's Rest is the one station in the Expanse where you can say you're broke and someone feeds you. That is worth something.
The dockmaster here owes me three favors from my last run through. I cleared two on the way in. Kept the last one in reserve.
The Guild bonded warehouses here run tight inventory. They count to the crate. I count to the kilogram.
I ran perimeter security here for two years before the company folded. Same corners, same sightlines. Memory like that does not fade.
Iron Depths. The ore here runs in seams twenty meters wide. I drilled three of them. The Union data underreports the yield by eight percent.
The Nova Research basement archives have not been indexed since 2314. I have a working catalog now. No one has asked for it yet.
Collective stations keep fuel costs within 4 percent of consortium average. It is one of the few pricing benchmarks that is actually enforced.
The Collective infrastructure documentation I have seen references the same construction project Marcus's father worked. The records go further back than I expected.
Union maintenance contracts specify torque tolerances to the newton-meter. Most outfitters ignore the spec. That is why their drives fail at the midpoint.
DCMC accident reports get filed under Union compliance review. I know because I have read the real ones. The version they publish is missing paragraphs.
The Guild requires inspection documentation at every transfer point. The forms are thorough. The inspectors are not.
Guild stations maintain public price records going back forty years. The data is clean. The analysis requires a different skill set.
Way I see it, the Union runs good stations and bad politics. The quality control on the machine shops is better than anything in Commerce Guild territory.
The Collective stations charge a research surcharge on every docked vessel. It shows up as station maintenance on the receipt. I have been reading receipts for years.
Free Alliance security contracts are verbal most of the time. The handshake culture means you need to know who is standing behind the person you shake with.
Free Alliance stations operate without central oversight. The way they stay functional is not something I have been able to fully model.
Collective network archives are indexed differently at each station. The cross-reference gaps are where the interesting data lives.
Alliance contracts run on trust and obligation. The risk premium is real, but so is the loyalty once you have held up your end.
Jin, you stripped the port thruster housing without filing a maintenance log. I cannot track service intervals on work nobody recorded.
Reeves, you filed a deviation report on my thruster swap. It passed certification. The deviation was unnecessary.
Priya, the sensor array you were running last night pulled 340 watts of unallocated power. That is 3.4 percent of our reserve capacity.
Elena, I am aware of the power draw. I factored it into the analysis window. The data required sustained observation.
Drifter, the contract window you agreed to on Stellaris gives us eleven days. If we detour through Alliance space that drops to six.
Reeves, the Alliance detour adds two contacts who can move our cargo at a 15-credit premium. Run those numbers against your six-day deadline.
Doc, your sample containers are not rated for the pressure differential in cargo bay three. I re-secured them. Do not put them back.
Marcus, I appreciate the re-securing. I was not aware bay three ran a different pressure cycle. That is useful operational data.
Drifter, you negotiated a three-day delivery window for a four-day route. Either the math works or it does not.
Marcus, the math works when you know which docks skip the queue for regular customers. I know two on the route. You are welcome.
Tomas, the cargo you logged as miscellaneous components appears on record as restricted biological precursors. I need an explanation.
Priya, the buyers know what they are buying. The classification is a paperwork issue, not a safety one.
Jin, your combat spacing on the last engagement was too tight. You were in my firing line twice. Do not let it be three.
Reeves, the hold manifest says forty crates. I count thirty-eight. Someone needs to resolve that before we dock.
Doc, you have been watching me work. I will assume professional curiosity. The answer is: careful, and I do not leave evidence.
You looked into the Uprising inheritance records. That data touches four Guild infrastructure contracts still active today. Worth knowing what connects them.
The inheritance records from the Uprising. I have seen that documentation before. The Section Four survivors' trust was folded into a Guild holding company within a year.
Silent Shaft. I know what the memorial leaves out. You have been there now. Some things you cannot read on a plaque.
The Silent Shaft ceremony was something else. Way I see it, places that remember their dead honestly are the ones that do not repeat the same mistakes.
The DCMC data you retrieved. I cross-referenced it against published incident reports. Twelve entries do not appear on the public record at all.
Those DCMC numbers you pulled. The gap in the safety maintenance contracts covers the same period as the Section Six collapse. That is not coincidence.
The NAS signals you tracked. Three of the pricing nodes on this route feed from the same antenna cluster. I have adjusted our timing windows accordingly.
The NAS intelligence data is consistent with what the Collective analysis suggested, though the Collective version was significantly redacted before distribution.
You know about my father's project. The full scope of it took me years to piece together. I am still not sure I have all of it.
What Marcus's father was working on. The infrastructure logs I have found connect to five of the current deep-route waypoints. The man built things meant to last.
Hull stress report from that engagement: four panel seams need re-welding, and the port thruster ran 11 percent over rated load for 40 seconds. I have logged it.
Good fight. Check the coupling on the dorsal gun mount before the next jump. I heard it rattle during the engagement.
I have documented the engagement parameters. The data on ship response time under sustained fire will inform my next set of recommendations.
We walked out. That is the only metric I care about when combat is over. Everything else is salvage.
Weapons ran clean. Hull took three hits. I want twenty minutes with the targeting system before we jump again.
I have been running fuel projections for the next three jumps. The margin is tighter than I would like. I will file the variance report tonight.
Preventative maintenance schedule is running six hours behind. Not ideal. I will cover it overnight.
I have been reviewing the Collective published data on wormhole transit effects. The Institute methodology is dated but the core findings hold up.
Way I see it, the best route is not always the fastest one. Sometimes it is the one with the best cantina at the midpoint.
We are carrying 4 tons of cargo that is earning nothing. I have flagged three buyers on the next approach vector.
Drive coupling on the starboard side is showing micro-fatigue. I checked the maintenance contract. It is within tolerance, but I am watching it.
The samples I have been running have one result I cannot explain. In my experience, unexplainable results are rarely nothing.
I have a contact on the next station who owes me a favor from three runs ago. Marcus, remind me not to forget.
I ran a threat assessment on the patrol vessels we passed. Three were undermanned. Good to know.
I keep a running log of crew health indicators. Nothing alarming. But some of the fatigue patterns are worth monitoring on longer routes.
